title: Protocol: constructing the Mizan Index, a composite measure of clinical research justice for African countries
body: Can a composite index grounded in Islamic ethical principles quantify research justice across African nations using registry data? We will query ClinicalTrials.gov API v2 for all interventional trials listing each of 20 African countries as a study location, retrieving total counts, condition-specific counts for the top four killers, phase distributions, sponsor metadata, status distributions, and multi-site indicators for up to 200 sample trials per country. Seven dimensions will be scored from zero to 14.3 each: Hifz al-Nafs via burden-trial alignment, Adl via per-capita density relative to the global median, Istikhlaf via local sponsor percentage, Shura via implementation-science trial share, La Darar via inverse ghost enrollment rate, Ilm via Phase 1 capacity and institutional diversity, and Amanah via inverse unknown-status rate. Scores will be summed into a composite Mizan Index ranging from zero to 100, with countries ranked and graded. Sensitivity analyses will vary dimension weights and ghost-enrollment thresholds. The primary outcome is a ranked scorecard with radar profiles identifying which justice dimensions are most deficient continent-wide. Limitations include restriction to one registry, keyword-based sponsor classification, and proxy measurement of community engagement.

sentences.text: Can a composite index grounded in Islamic ethical principles quantify research justice across African nations using publicly available clinical trial registry data?

We will query ClinicalTrials.gov API v2 for all interventional trials listing each of 20 African countries as a study location, retrieving total counts, condition-specific counts, phase distributions, sponsor metadata, status distributions, and multi-site indicators.

Seven dimensions will be scored from zero to 14.3 each: Hifz al-Nafs via burden-trial alignment, Adl via per-capita density, Istikhlaf via local sponsorship, Shura via implementation-science share, La Darar via inverse ghost enrollment, Ilm via Phase 1 capacity and institutional diversity, and Amanah via inverse unknown-status rate.
Dimension scores will be summed into a composite Mizan Index ranging from zero to 100, with countries ranked, graded, and profiled using radar charts to visualise dimension-level balance.
Sensitivity analyses will vary dimension weights using equal and burden-prioritised schemes and test ghost-enrollment thresholds at 20, 50, and 100 sites.
The primary outcome is a ranked scorecard with radar profiles identifying which justice dimensions are most deficient continent-wide, providing actionable targets for research policy.

Limitations include restriction to a single registry, keyword-based sponsor classification that may misclassify some institutions, and proxy measurement of community engagement through phase designation rather than direct community participation data.

outsideNote.data: ClinicalTrials.gov API v2 (planned), 20 African countries, WHO GBD, UN population data

ai_transparency: LLM assistance was used for drafting and language editing. The author reviewed and edited the manuscript and takes responsibility for the final content.
